solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
1431C - Black Friday
Let's consider the most optimal set of items of some fixed size $$$x$$$. We want the cheapest items to be as expensive as possible, so it's easy to see that taking $$$x$$$ most expensive items overall works the best.
The other observation that makes it easier to code is that you only have to consider such $$$x$$$ that are multiples of $$$k$$$. If you take some $$$x$$$ that is not multiple of $$$k$$$, then you will only make $$$\lfloor \frac x k \rfloor$$$ cheapest items cheaper compared to the nearest multiple below it.
So the solution is to iterate over multiples of $$$k$$$ and choose the best answer of them.
Overall complexity: $$$O(n^2)$$$ per testcase.

solutions.title: Solution (elizarov)
solutions.content:
fun main
()
{
repeat
(
readLine
()!!.
toInt
())
{
val
(
n
,
k
)
=
readLine
()!!.
split
(
" "
).
map
{
it
.
toInt
()
}
val p
=
readLine
()!!.
split
(
" "
).
map
{
it
.
toInt
()
}
val answer
=
(
1.
.
n
/
k
).
maxOf
{
d
->
val m
=
d
*
k
            p
.
subList
(
n
-
m
,
n
-
m
+
d
).
sum
()
}
println
(
answer
)
}
}
